title: Managerial Decision Making
description: Learn how managers use financial and non-financial information to make structured business decisions. This module covers relevant costs, opportunity costs, sunk costs, incremental analysis, make-or-buy decisions, special orders, product mix decisions, outsourcing, capacity constraints, risk, and qualitative considerations.

sections.title: Learning Objectives
items: Understand the role of managerial decision making in business economics.
Distinguish between relevant and irrelevant information.
Explain the importance of incremental revenues and incremental costs.
Identify opportunity costs, avoidable costs, and sunk costs.
Apply relevant-cost analysis to practical business decisions.
Evaluate special orders, make-or-buy decisions, outsourcing, and product discontinuation.
Understand how capacity constraints influence product mix decisions.
Recognize the importance of qualitative factors, risk, and strategic consequences.

sections.title: Introduction
content: Managers make decisions continuously. They choose which products to offer, whether to outsource activities, how to use limited resources, and whether to accept unusual customer orders.
Managerial decision making is the process of identifying alternatives, evaluating their consequences, and selecting the option that best supports the organization's objectives.
Good decisions require more than accounting data. Managers must combine financial analysis with operational knowledge, strategic judgment, and an understanding of risk.

sections.title: A Structured Decision-Making Process
content: A structured decision usually begins by defining the problem clearly.
Managers then identify realistic alternatives, estimate the relevant consequences of each alternative, compare the alternatives, and choose a course of action.
After implementation, the results should be monitored to determine whether the decision achieved its objectives.

sections.title: Example: Choosing a Maintenance Strategy
content: A shipping company must decide whether to continue repairing an older auxiliary engine or replace it with a more efficient unit.
Management should compare the future repair costs, fuel savings, installation cost, expected downtime, reliability, and remaining vessel life.
The original purchase price of the old engine does not change between the alternatives and should not determine the decision.

sections.title: Relevant Information
content: Information is relevant to a decision when it concerns the future and differs between the available alternatives.
A cost or revenue that remains the same regardless of the decision is normally irrelevant.
Relevant analysis therefore focuses on the financial consequences that will change because one alternative is selected instead of another.

sections.title: Incremental Analysis
content: Incremental analysis compares the additional revenues and additional costs created by a decision.
Incremental revenue is the extra revenue generated by choosing one alternative.
Incremental cost is the extra cost caused by that alternative.
An alternative is financially attractive when its incremental benefits exceed its incremental costs, assuming qualitative factors are also acceptable.

sections.title: Example: Incremental Profit
content: A company can accept an additional order that generates $120,000 in revenue.
The order requires $75,000 of additional materials, labor, and delivery costs.
The incremental profit is $45,000.
Existing fixed administrative costs are irrelevant if they will not change because of the order.

sections.title: Relevant Costs
content: Relevant costs are future costs that differ between alternatives.
Examples may include additional materials, additional labor, avoidable maintenance, external supplier prices, and additional transport costs.

The classification of a cost as fixed or variable is not enough by itself. A fixed cost can be relevant if it changes, while a variable cost can be irrelevant if it remains identical under all alternatives.

sections.title: Avoidable and Unavoidable Costs
content: An avoidable cost can be eliminated by choosing a particular alternative.
An unavoidable cost will continue regardless of the decision.
Only avoidable costs normally create financial benefits when an activity, product, or department is discontinued.

sections.title: Example: Avoidable Fixed Cost
content: A company considers closing a regional warehouse.
The warehouse manager's salary would disappear, making it avoidable and relevant.
A share of head-office rent allocated to the warehouse would continue even after closure, making it unavoidable and irrelevant to the decision.

sections.title: Sunk Costs
content: A sunk cost is a cost that has already been incurred and cannot be changed by any current decision.
Sunk costs are irrelevant because they are identical under every future alternative.
Managers sometimes allow past expenditures to influence decisions, even when doing so leads to additional losses.

sections.title: The Sunk Cost Trap
content: A common mistake is continuing an unsuccessful project because the company has already invested heavily in it.
The correct question is not how much has already been spent.
The correct question is whether the expected future benefits justify the expected future costs from this point onward.

sections.title: Opportunity Costs
content: An opportunity cost is the benefit sacrificed when one alternative is chosen instead of another.
Opportunity costs are often missing from the accounting records, but they can be highly relevant.
They are especially important when labor, machinery, vessel capacity, warehouse space, or capital is limited.

sections.title: Shipping Example: Opportunity Cost
content: A vessel has limited cargo capacity and can carry either Cargo A or Cargo B.
Choosing Cargo A means sacrificing the contribution that could have been earned from Cargo B.
The forgone contribution from Cargo B is an opportunity cost of accepting Cargo A.

sections.title: Special Order Decisions
content: A special order is a one-time customer order offered at an unusual price or under unusual conditions.
When unused capacity is available, the minimum financially acceptable price may be based primarily on incremental costs.
However, managers must also consider whether the order will affect normal customers, market prices, brand positioning, quality, or future negotiations.

sections.title: Example: Special Order With Spare Capacity
content: A manufacturer normally sells a product for $100 per unit.
A new customer offers $72 per unit for a one-time order.
The variable cost is $55 per unit, and no additional fixed costs are required.
The order creates an incremental contribution of $17 per unit and may be financially attractive if spare capacity exists and normal sales are unaffected.

sections.title: Special Orders at Full Capacity
content: When a company is operating at full capacity, accepting a special order may displace regular sales.
The contribution lost from those regular sales becomes an opportunity cost.
A price that is acceptable under spare capacity may therefore be unacceptable when capacity is constrained.

sections.title: Make-or-Buy Decisions
content: A make-or-buy decision asks whether a company should produce a component or service internally or purchase it from an external supplier.
The relevant comparison includes avoidable internal production costs, the supplier's price, opportunity costs, and any additional costs created by outsourcing.
Allocated fixed overhead should not automatically be treated as avoidable.

sections.title: Example: Make or Buy
content: An internal component has a reported cost of $40 per unit, including $12 of allocated fixed overhead.
Only $3 of the fixed overhead would disappear if production stopped.
The relevant internal cost is therefore not $40. It consists of the variable production costs plus the $3 avoidable fixed cost and any opportunity cost of using the facilities.

sections.title: Qualitative Factors in Outsourcing
content: The cheapest supplier is not always the best alternative.

Managers should consider quality, delivery reliability, technical competence, information security, regulatory compliance, supplier dependence, employee effects, and the strategic importance of the activity.
Outsourcing a critical capability may create long-term risks that are not visible in a simple cost comparison.

sections.title: Keep-or-Drop Decisions
content: Managers may consider discontinuing an unprofitable product, service, customer, vessel, or department.
The relevant question is whether the contribution margin lost would be greater or smaller than the avoidable fixed costs saved.
A segment showing an accounting loss may still contribute positively toward unavoidable common costs.

sections.title: Example: Discontinuing a Service
content: A service line generates contribution margin of $300,000 but is allocated $420,000 of fixed costs.
Only $180,000 of those fixed costs would disappear if the service were discontinued.
Closing the service would remove $300,000 of contribution but save only $180,000, reducing total company profit by $120,000.

sections.title: Product Mix Under a Capacity Constraint
content: When a scarce resource limits production, managers should not rank products only by contribution margin per unit.
Instead, products should normally be evaluated by contribution margin per unit of the constrained resource.
The constrained resource may be machine hours, labor hours, production capacity, warehouse space, or cargo capacity.

sections.title: Example: Contribution per Machine Hour
content: Product A earns contribution margin of $60 and requires three machine hours, giving $20 per machine hour.
Product B earns contribution margin of $45 and requires one machine hour, giving $45 per machine hour.
When machine hours are the limiting factor, Product B should normally receive priority.

sections.title: Replacement Decisions
content: Replacement decisions compare the future costs and benefits of keeping an existing asset with those of acquiring a new one.
The book value of the old asset is normally a sunk cost.
Relevant factors include future operating costs, maintenance, downtime, resale value, acquisition cost, productivity, risk, and expected useful life.

sections.title: Shipping Example: Replace or Continue Operating
content: A vessel owner is considering replacing an older cargo-handling crane.
The analysis should include future repairs, lost operating time, safety risk, resale value, installation cost, and the efficiency of the new crane.
The historical purchase cost and accumulated depreciation of the old crane do not change because of the decision.

sections.title: Risk and Uncertainty
content: Decision models often rely on estimates of future prices, demand, costs, operating conditions, and customer behavior.
These estimates may be wrong.
Managers can use sensitivity analysis, scenario analysis, probabilities, and ranges of outcomes to understand how uncertainty affects the recommendation.

sections.title: Example: Sensitivity Analysis
content: A fuel-efficiency investment appears profitable if fuel prices remain high.
Management recalculates the result under high, medium, and low fuel-price scenarios.
This analysis shows whether the decision remains attractive when an important assumption changes.

sections.title: Non-Financial and Strategic Considerations
content: Not every important consequence can be measured accurately in money.
Managers may need to consider employee safety, customer satisfaction, environmental performance, regulatory compliance, reputation, resilience, and long-term competitive position.
A decision that improves short-term profit can still damage the company strategically.

sections.title: Expert Insight
content: High-quality managerial decisions separate relevant information from accounting noise. The most difficult part is often not the calculation, but identifying which costs, revenues, constraints, risks, and strategic consequences truly change between alternatives.

sections.title: Common Mistakes
content: Including sunk costs in the decision.
Assuming all allocated fixed costs will disappear.
Ignoring opportunity costs when capacity is limited.
Accepting a low-priced order without considering displaced sales.
Choosing the lowest-cost supplier without evaluating quality and strategic risk.
Focusing only on short-term profit.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
items: Managerial decision making compares realistic alternatives and their consequences.
Relevant information concerns the future and differs between alternatives.
Incremental analysis focuses on additional revenues and additional costs.
Sunk costs are irrelevant, while opportunity costs can be highly relevant.
Avoidable costs matter in outsourcing and discontinuation decisions.
Special orders must be evaluated differently depending on available capacity.
When a resource is constrained, managers should focus on contribution per unit of the scarce resource.
Financial analysis should always be combined with risk, qualitative factors, and strategic judgment.

sections.title: Reflection
questions: Why can an allocated fixed cost be misleading in a make-or-buy decision?
How does limited capacity change the minimum acceptable price for a special order?
Why might a company keep a product or service that reports an accounting loss?
Which qualitative factors would you consider before outsourcing vessel maintenance to a new supplier?
